text: The surgeon enters the retrorectus space on the right side to perform an ETEP approach for a complex incisional hernia repair. The surgical field shows the anatomical area affected by previous procedures, including a left-sided colostomy and Hartmann reversal.
The surgeon enters the retrorectus space on the right side using an 8mm robotic trocar with gas insufflation, avoiding the left side due to the patient's previous surgeries.

The surgeon uses an 8mm robotic trocar with gas insufflation to dissect the retrorectus space, cleaning areolar tissue off the posterior sheath while moving side to side, aiming towards the right groin.

The surgeon uses insufflation to expand the retrorectus space, dissecting side to side to clean areolar tissue from the posterior sheath, progressing towards the right groin. Upon reaching the port's limit, the cannula and camera are removed to complete the dissection with camera assistance.

The surgeon removes the cannula and camera after reaching the maximum extent of the port, then continues with camera dissection, maneuvering the camera to achieve further dissection in the retrorectus space.

The surgeon uses a five millimeter zero degree scope to perform camera dissection, creating space for the middle quadrant port. The scope is maneuvered to facilitate further inferior dissection, preparing the surgical field for additional instrument use.

The surgeon uses a five millimeter zero degree scope to create room for the middle quadrant port, entering the skin lateral to the semilunar line marked pre-operatively with ultrasound, and approaching the lateral border of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon uses a spinal needle to enter the skin lateral to the semilunar line, marked pre-operatively with ultrasound, and proceeds to the lateral border of the rectus muscle. The robotic system is docked, with scissors in the left hand and a bipolar instrument in the right, to clean the retro-rectus space.

The surgeon docks the robot, positioning scissors in the left hand and a bipolar instrument in the right hand, to clean the retro-rectus space. The procedure involves a lower midline crossover, with the surgeon maneuvering the instruments to navigate the angles effectively from the right side.

The surgeon performs a limited crossover dissection in the lower midline, initially concerned about the angles with the right hand. The dissection progresses into the left groin, followed by an attempt to cross the midline in the upper region.

The surgeon navigates the camera in the left upper quadrant, observing omental adhesions. Despite the trocar being positioned in the right lower quadrant, the surgeon successfully crosses the midline above, demonstrating effective maneuverability.

The surgeon uses a camera placed in the left upper quadrant to inspect the surgical field, observing predominantly omental adhesions. The trocar positioned in the right lower quadrant allows for effective visualization and crossing of the midline.
The surgeon reaches the upper extent of the hernia and enters the left retrorectus space in the high left upper quadrant to create more room for the procedure.
The surgeon reaches the upper extent of the hernia and enters the left retrorectus space in the high left upper quadrant to create more room for the procedure.

The surgeon opens the entire right posterior rectus sheath, carefully clearing adhesions on the peritoneal side before proceeding. Using the left hand, the surgeon sweeps and creates a tunnel, successfully opening the sheath down below the arcuate line with minimal difficulty. The hernia defect is visible medially.

The surgeon carefully opens the right posterior rectus sheath, ensuring adhesions on the peritoneal side are cleared before proceeding. Using the left hand, a tunnel is created, allowing the sheath to be opened below the arcuate line with minimal difficulty. The hernia defect remains visible medially.
The surgeon carefully reduces the peritoneum while preserving the posterior elements, assessing the need for a transversus abdominis release due to the hernia's growth.
The surgeon carefully reduces the peritoneum while preserving the posterior elements, assessing the need for a transversus abdominis release due to the hernia's growth.
The surgeon continues to reduce the peritoneum and hernia sac while approaching the scarring area of the left posterior rectus sheath, where the colostomy was previously located.
The surgeon continues to reduce the peritoneum and hernia sac, approaching the area of scarring from a previous colostomy on the left posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon uses cautery to confidently dissect and preserve the hernia sac, navigating behind it to separate it from surrounding tissue. The intraperitoneal view shows minimal adhesions, allowing for smooth progress in reducing the defect in the posterior elements.

The surgeon uses cautery to confidently dissect and preserve the hernia sac, moving into the plane behind it. The intraperitoneal view shows no concerning adhesions, allowing for careful manipulation of the posterior elements.

The surgeon identifies Prolene stitches from the colostomy site closure and confirms the absence of a hernia in this location. They carefully cauterize between the posterior and anterior sheaths, ensuring no bowel is present beneath. The surgeon skives into the posterior sheath, detaching it from the back of the muscle while avoiding defects in the anterior sheath or muscle.

The surgeon identifies Prolene stitches from the colostomy site closure, confirming the absence of a hernia in this location. While cauterizing between the posterior and anterior sheaths, the surgeon ensures no bowel is present beneath, minimizing concerns about thermal spread. The surgeon carefully dissects into the posterior sheath, avoiding defects in the anterior sheath or muscle.

The surgeon performs a quick check of the bowel to ensure its integrity, then continues taking down the remaining hernia sac. The dissection reveals a small bridge of the inferior portion of the left posterior rectus sheath. Below the colostomy closure, the surgeon encounters a virgin plane and finishes removing scarred adherence from the colostomy site, carefully avoiding lateral holes in the posterior sheath. The dissection progresses to the opposite side, concluding with inferior dissection.

The surgeon performs a quick check of the bowel, ensuring its integrity before proceeding to take down the remaining hernia sac. The dissection reveals a small bridge of the inferior portion of the left posterior rectus sheath. Below the colostomy closure, the surgeon encounters a virgin plane, finishing the dissection of scarred adherence from the colostomy site while avoiding lateral holes in the posterior sheath. The dissection is completed inferiorly.

A lot of times when I've done these cases, I end up placing a trocar in the contralateral upper quadrant and then docking from above aiming down. In the end, it wouldn't have been a bad idea for me to do that because I ended up placing the mesh through a twelve millimeter trocar that I had upsized the original left upper quadrant five millimeter trocar for. So it would have been free to do that anyway, upsize that in order to place the mesh at the end of the case. But I really did want to try to do this whole dissection just from the lateral side as I haven't done that as many times. And in the end, I actually think it went pretty nicely. I did have concerns doing this from the right side just due to the angles being right-handed, but in the end, it really didn't interfere that much with a good operation.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the left posterior rectus sheath and the left retrorectus space below the arcuate line, using a trocar placed in the left upper quadrant that was upsized from five millimeters to twelve millimeters to facilitate mesh placement.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the left posterior rectus sheath and the retrorectus space below the arcuate line. The hernia defect is assessed, and a transversus abdominis release (TAR) is deemed necessary. The surgeon begins creating a space in the left lower quadrant below the arcuate line for the TAR procedure.

The surgeon examines the hernia defect and begins a transversus abdominis release (TAR) in the left lower quadrant, below the arcuate line. The dissection progresses upwards, avoiding holes near the insertion of neurovascular bundles and the site of the previous trocar. The surgeon carefully navigates the muscular portion of the transversus abdominis (TA) while closing any inadvertent holes made during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully avoids creating additional holes while dissecting in the upper abdomen, near the site of the five millimeter trocar. The dissection continues in a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) approach, staying medial to the neurovascular bundles. The surgeon progresses towards the muscular portion of the transversus abdominis, marking from the upper abdomen and entering the plane to facilitate dissection. A small hole is made, which is visible and will be closed.

The surgeon marks the upper abdomen and enters the plane from a top-down approach, using the angles of the trocars to facilitate the completion of the dissection. A small hole is visible, which the surgeon proceeds to close.

The surgeon completes the transversus abdominis release (TAR) and performs lateral mobilization of the peritoneum, extending the dissection superiorly and inferiorly beneath the ribs. The space of Retzius is developed slightly to allow for mesh placement, avoiding holes in the peritoneum.

The surgeon completes the transversus abdominis release (TAR) and performs lateral mobilization of the peritoneum, extending the dissection laterally from superior to inferior. The space of Retzius is partially developed to allow for mesh placement. The surgeon then closes a hole in the posterior sheath with minimal tension.
The surgeon closes a hole in the posterior sheath with minimal tension, using a 3-0 V-lock suture in a running fashion to close additional holes on the left-hand side.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to close holes on the left-hand side in a running fashion, reducing pressure to six. The suture is knotted, prompting the surgeon to switch from closing from below to starting from the top. The surgeon uses a zero, one eighty V-lock, taking several throws and cinching every few throws to imbricate the tissue.

The surgeon reduces the intra-abdominal pressure to six and begins closing the diastatic portion of the linea alba using an 18-inch V-lock suture. The rectus muscles are approximated in the midline, and the closure of the main hernia defect progresses smoothly. The surgeon alternates between suturing from above and below, imbricating the hernia sac as they proceed. The closure is completed by cutting both the superior and inferior stitches and starting a new V-lock suture from the center to address the residual hernia defect.

The surgeon reduces the pressure to six and progressively tightens the sutures, bringing the rectus muscles together in the midline. The closure of the main hernia defect proceeds smoothly. The surgeon continues imbricating the hernia from below after running out of stitch from above.

The surgeon palpates the midline, noting a ridge formed by the outward bowing of the imbricated hernia sac. The superior and inferior stitches are cut, and a new eighteen-inch V-lock suture is initiated from the center to close the remaining hernia defect.

The surgeon runs a suture along the hernia repair site, noting the minimal tension as the tissue closes smoothly. The suture is extended up to meet the end of the superior stitch, and then the surgeon begins to run it back down.

The surgeon runs the suture up to the point where the superior stitch ended, crossing it, and then proceeds to run the suture back down, observing minimal tension as the closure comes together smoothly.

The surgeon measures the surgical space, noting a lateral width of twenty-seven centimeters and a superior to inferior length of thirty centimeters. A thirty by thirty centimeter heavyweight mesh is trimmed to twenty-six centimeters in width with rounded corners. The mesh is introduced through a twelve millimeter trocar in the left upper quadrant and positioned down to the space of Retzius.

The surgeon measures the surgical space, noting dimensions of twenty-seven centimeters in lateral width and thirty centimeters anterior-posterior. A piece of heavyweight mesh, originally thirty by thirty centimeters, is trimmed to twenty-six centimeters in width with rounded corners. A twelve millimeter trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant to facilitate placement of the mesh into the space of Retzius.
The surgeon positions the mesh without fixation, using ports from the right lower quadrant to the right upper quadrant. A drain is inserted, and the abdomen is desufflated under direct vision.
The surgeon positions the mesh without fixation, using a port in the right lower quadrant to pull a drain towards the right upper quadrant. The surgical field is desufflated under direct vision.
